nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still waters including swamps, marshes, floodplains, and densely vegetated lowland rivers and ponds; typically found near the surface among floating and emergent vegetation

decorPreferences: floating plants (water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) for surface cover and bubble-nest anchoring
dense background planting (vallisneria, water wisteria, tall Hygrophila)
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and hiding spots
subdued lighting or floating cover to reduce skittishness

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods. Offer high-quality flake or pellet as a staple alongside regular frozen or live foods: bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and mosquito larvae. The Moonlight Gourami will also graze on soft aquatic plants and algae. Feed small amounts once or twice daily, and vary the diet to support good colouration and breeding condition.

avoidWith: nippy barbs (tiger barb) — will nip the trailing ventral filaments
small nano fish that may be bullied or eaten

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by H. Zell, 5580x3692. Sharp, well-lit lateral shot of a vibrant silvery-blue individual clearly showing the diagnostic orange-red ventral (pelvic) filaments; whole fish in frame against dark background.
Photo by Greg Hume (Greg5030), 5184x3456. Crisp in-tank shot showing the glowing silver-white body and long trailing ventral filaments; colourless filaments (non-nuptial/female).
Photo by Haplochromis, 1066x804. Silvery body against dense green planting for a planted-tank context; correct ID, slightly lower resolution.

summary: The Moonlight Gourami is a large, silver-bodied labyrinth fish from Southeast Asia, reaching up to 15 cm in adulthood. Males develop a distinctive orange-red ventral filament and build bubble nests among floating plants. Generally peaceful in community settings, though males tend to be territorial with one another.
careNotes: The Moonlight Gourami, known in older literature under the name Trichogaster microlepis, is one of the larger gourami species, with adults reaching 12–15 cm. Its uniform silver-white colouration, which appears to glow under aquarium lighting, gives it its common name. Males are distinguished by orange-red ventral (pelvic) filaments; females’ filaments are colourless or pale yellow. A tank of at least 150 L with a minimum 100 cm footprint is recommended to give adults adequate swimming space and a territorial buffer. As a labyrinth fish, the Moonlight Gourami breathes atmospheric air directly at the water’s surface — the gap between the waterline and the lid should be kept warm and humid to prevent cold air from chilling the labyrinth organ, and cold draughts near the tank should be avoided. Floating plants are strongly recommended both to anchor bubble nests during breeding and to reduce the fish’s natural skittishness in open, exposed conditions. Males tend to be aggressive toward other males and toward other large gourami species; keeping a single male or a single true pair is advisable in most community setups. The species is less tolerant of cool temperatures than some other gouramis, so warm, slightly soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water should be maintained. The older name Trichogaster microlepis remains widely used in hobbyist literature alongside the current scientific name.
breedingNotes: The Moonlight Gourami is a bubble-nest builder. Condition a pair with live and frozen foods for one to two weeks before introducing them to a dedicated breeding tank of at least 80–120 L, with a water depth of around 25–30 cm, floating plants, and very gentle filtration (a sponge filter works well). The male constructs a substantial bubble nest under the floating vegetation and courts the female with lateral displays. After an embrace-style spawning, the male guards and tends the nest aggressively; the female should be removed at this point to prevent injury. Eggs hatch within 24–48 hours and the fry become free-swimming after 3–4 days. Feed the fry on infusoria or commercial fry food initially, transitioning to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp as they grow. Successful breeding generally requires a separate setup with the specific water conditions and floating cover this species relies on.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Trichopodus microlepis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 15 cm), temperature range (24–30°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–18°H), native range (Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam), habitat (floodplains, swamps, still vegetated waters), diet, breeding behaviour (bubble-nester, embrace spawning, paternal nest guarding), taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), and synonym Trichogaster microlepis.

additional.name: FishBase — Trichopodus microlepis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 15 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C reported; wider tolerance), native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam), taxonomy, and labyrinth organ confirmation. pH and hardness parameters aligned with SeriouslyFish. FishBase upper temperature 28°C; SeriouslyFish cites 30°C for breeding condition — used 30°C as max per trade consensus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Gourami Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-facing care guidance used to inform tank size recommendations (minimum 30 gallons / ~115 L for larger gouramis), diet (accepts flake, pellets, frozen foods), compatibility notes (semi-aggressive toward own species; avoid fin-nippers), and labyrinth-organ surface-breathing note. Tank minimum rounded up to 150 L for a species reaching 15 cm per playbook guidance.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Moonlight Gourami profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for sexual dimorphism detail (male orange-red ventral filaments vs. female pale/colourless filaments), skittishness in open tanks, floating-plant recommendation for stress reduction, and bubble-nest breeding notes including male aggression toward female post-spawning and recommendation to remove female.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder. A separate breeding tank with floating plants, shallow water (around 25–30 cm), and minimal flow is required. The male guards the nest after spawning, and the female should be removed at that point to prevent injury. Fry need infusoria or commercial fry food initially, progressing to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp.
